turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the Court in case 24-316, Kennedy v. Braidwood Management.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: This case concerns the Appointments Clause in Article 2 of the Constitution. The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force is a government entity within the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, or HHS. The task force issues public recommendations about preventive health care services, for example, recommendations about cancer screenings, diabetes testing, preventive heart medications, and the like. Before 2010, the task force's recommendations were purely advisory, but the Affordable Care Act of 2010 now mandates that health insurers cover some of the task force's recommended services at no cost to the insured. The Secretary of Health and Human Services has appointed the 16 current members of the task force who make the recommendations about covered preventive health services. The plaintiffs in this case argue that the appointment of the task force members by the Secretary of HHS is not consistent with the Appointments Clause in Article 2 of the Constitution. They contend that the task force members must be appointed by the president with the advice and consent of the senate. The government under President Biden and now also under President Trump disagrees with the plaintiffs. The government contends that the appointment of the task force members by the Secretary of HHS is permissible under the constitution. We agree with the government. The Appointments Clause issue here ultimately turns on whether the members of the task force are principal officers or inferior officers for purposes of the constitution. The text of the Appointments Clause in Article 2 divides officers in the Executive Branch into two categories, principal officers and inferior officers. Principal officers must be appointed by the president with the advice and consent of the senate. Inferior officers may also be appointed through presidential nomination and senate confirmation if Congress chooses that method of appointment. But the framers recognize that requiring all officers of the Executive Branch to run the gauntlet of presidential nomination and senate confirmation would prove administratively unworkable as offices became numerous and sudden removals and prompt replacements became necessary. So on one of the last days of the Constitutional Convention, September 15, 1787, they authorized an additional and streamlined method of appointment for inferior officers. Specifically, as relevant here, Article 2 provides that Congress by law may vest appointment of inferior officers directly in the heads of departments such as here by the Secretary of HHS instead of appointment by presidential nomination and senate confirmation. So how does a court determine whether executive officers, such as members of the task force, are principal officers who must be appointed by the president with senate confirmation, or instead are inferior officers and therefore may be appointed by a head of department alone, such as in this case by the Secretary of HHS? Inferior officers, the Court has said, are those whose work is supervised and directed not by the president directly, but rather supervised and directed by other officers who themselves were appointed by the president with the advice and consent of the senate. To oversimplify, for purposes of explanation, does the officer in question report directly to the president or does the officer instead report to someone else below the president in the chain of command, such as the Secretary of HHS. Here, task force members are inferior officers because they in effect report to the Secretary of HHS. The task force's work is supervised and directed by the Secretary of HHS. The secretary's ability to supervise and direct the task force derives from two main sources. First, the secretary has the authority to remove task force members at will. This Court has previously recognized that the authority to remove an officer at will carries with it some power to supervise and direct that officer. And second, the Secretary of HHS has statutory authority to review and block the task force's recommendations before they can take effect. In other words, the task force's recommendations are not immediately binding on insurers but the secretary has a period of time, at least one year, the statute says, to review and block the recommendations if the secretary chooses to do so. Given those two mutually reinforcing means by which the Secretary of HHS can supervise and direct the task force, this Court's precedents dictate the conclusion that the task force members are inferior officers. In particular, as we explain in our opinion today, the Court's prior decisions in three cases known as Edmond, Free Enterprise Fund and Arthrex. In short, the task force members are inferior officers who may be appointed by the Secretary of HHS. The plaintiffs in this case also advance a second argument. They contend that even if task force members are inferior officers, meaning that Congress could provide for the Secretary of HHS to appoint the task force that Congress has not actually given the Secretary of HHS the authority of to appoint the task force members. We disagree with the plaintiffs on that argument as well. Congress has in two steps expressly vested the Secretary of HHS with the authority to appoint task force members. In 1999, when Congress first codified the task force, Congress authorized the Director of the Agency for Health Care Research and Quality within HHS to appoint members of the task force, and a law known as Reorganization Plan No. 3, which was passed by Congress and signed into law by President Reagan in 1984, transfers all of that director's functions to the Secretary of HHS. Those transferred functions now include the authority to appoint task force members. Therefore, by virtue of those two statutes, the Secretary of HHS has statutory authority to appoint the task force members. So the task force members have been appointed by the Secretary of HHS consistent with law and with the Appointments Clause in Article 2 of the Constitution. The manner of appointing task force members preserves the chain of political accountability that was central to the framers' design of the Appointments Clause. The task force members were appointed by and are supervised and directed by the Secretary of HHS, and the Secretary of HHS in turn answers to the President of the United States, consistent with Article 2 of the Constitution. For those and other reasons explained in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit and remand the case for further proceedings consistent with our opinion. Justice Thomas has filed the dissenting opinion which Justice Alito and Justice Gorsuch have joined.
